title: Chapter 7 Master Quiz
description: Welcome to Object-Oriented Programming. Test your knowledge of Classes, Objects, and Constructors.

questions.question: In Java, what is the best real-world analogy for a `Class`?
questions.explanation: A Class is just a design document (or blueprint). It doesn't physically exist in memory until you build an Object out of it.

questions.question: What is an `Object` (or Instance) in Java?
options: The file where you type your code.
The physical entity built in RAM based on the rules of a Class.
questions.explanation: Objects are the physical entities created ('instantiated') from your Class blueprints.

questions.question: Which keyword is used to take a Class blueprint and physically construct a new Object into memory?
questions.explanation: The `new` keyword allocates memory and builds the instance.

questions.question: If you define a Class called `Dog`, which line correctly creates a new Dog object?
questions.explanation: Type Name = new Type();

questions.question: What is an 'Attribute' (also called an Instance Variable or Field)?
options: A method that performs an action.
A piece of data or 'State' stored internally inside the object.
questions.explanation: Attributes define the distinct properties of an object (e.g., color, age, name).

questions.question: If you construct two separate Objects from the same Class, changing the internal data of Object A will also change the internal data of Object B because they share the same blueprint.
questions.explanation: Objects are completely independent of each other. Once built, their data is theirs alone.

questions.question: Which symbol do you use to access a specific Object's attributes?
questions.explanation: Dot notation (`myDog.name`) reaches inside the object to access its contents.

questions.question: What defines the 'Behavior' of an Object?
questions.explanation: Instance Methods (e.g., `dog.bark()`) define the actions an object can perform.

questions.question: Inside an Instance Method, the method can freely access and modify the Object's own internal Attributes without needing them passed in as parameters.
questions.explanation: Instance Methods are 'Self-Aware'. They sit inside the Object and have full access to its internal State.

questions.question: What is a 'Constructor'?
options: A keyword that deletes the object.
A special method that automatically runs exactly once when an object is created, used to initialize it.
A software pattern used in older languages.
questions.explanation: Constructors set up the object's initial data right as it's being born.

questions.question: What is one strict rule regarding Constructor names?
options: They must be named exactly identically to the Class name.
questions.explanation: If the class is `Car`, the constructor MUST be called `Car(...)`.

questions.question: What return type must a Constructor have?
options: Constructors do NOT have a return type, not even void.
questions.explanation: Because their built-in job is to return the newly constructed Object, you don't declare a return type for them in Java.

questions.question: If you explicitly define a custom Constructor `Dog(String name)`, what happens to the default empty `new Dog()` constructor?
options: Java deletes it. Attempting `new Dog()` will cause a compiler error because you are now FORCED to provide a name.
It sets the name to 'null' automatically.
Java throws a warning but complies.
questions.correctAnswer: Java deletes it. Attempting `new Dog()` will cause a compiler error because you are now FORCED to provide a name.
questions.explanation: Once you define custom Constructor rules, Java strictly enforces them.

questions.question: The keyword used to clarify that a variable is referring to the current Object Instance itself is `____`.
questions.explanation: `this.name` means 'the internal property named name belonging to me'.

questions.question: Why is `this.name = name;` so common inside Constructors?
options: To explicitly map the incoming Parameter (`name`) into the Object's internal Attribute (`this.name`), resolving the naming collision.
Because Java refuses to compile otherwise.
questions.explanation: Since developers prefer to name parameters the same as attributes, `this.` ensures Java knows exactly which `name` is which.

questions.question:
What prints?
`class Dog { String name; Dog(String n){name=n;} }`
`Dog d1 = new Dog("Max");`
`Dog d2 = new Dog("Buster");`
`System.out.println(d1.name);`
questions.explanation: d1 holds "Max", d2 holds "Buster". They are independent. d1.name prints Max.

questions.question: A class can have multiple attributes of varying data types (like an int, a String, and a boolean all inside a Dog).
questions.explanation: Yes, objects are complex storage boxes that can hold as many primitive variables or other objects as you want.

questions.question: If an instance method is called `public void run()`, running `myDog.run()` instructs...
options: Only the `myDog` instance to execute its run logic.
Every Dog instance globally to run.
questions.explanation: Dot notation commands the specific object instance entirely.

questions.question: Can two different Objects from two entirely different Classes both have an attribute named `health`?
options: Yes, variable names only need to be unique within their own Class Scope.
Only if one is public and the other is private.
questions.explanation: A Player class can have `health` and an Enemy class can have `health`. They are separated.

questions.question: If we don't define ANY constructors in our class, how does `new Dog()` work?
options: Java provides a completely invisible 'default empty constructor' automatically behind the scenes.
It asks the user to provide one.
questions.correctAnswer: Java provides a completely invisible 'default empty constructor' automatically behind the scenes.
questions.explanation: If you don't write one, Java gives you a free empty constructor so instantiation works.

questions.question: Taking a Class blueprint and creating an Object from it using `new` is technically termed "____stantiation".
questions.explanation: The term is Instantiation (building an Instance).
